title: Acts 25
subtitle: Paul Before Festus; Appeal to Caesar

sections.header: Verses 1—12 — Paul Appeals to Caesar Before Festus

heb.paragraph: Paul's appeal in v.11 is the formal legal term provocatio ad Caesarem — available to Roman citizens who believed they were being denied justice in the provinces. Once invoked before a magistrate, the appeal was irrevocable. Paul uses the one legal mechanism available to ensure he will reach Rome.

v.10 — Paul insists he is already standing at Caesar's court (Felix and Festus govern as Caesar's representatives). The appeal is not an admission of guilt but an assertion of his right to a fair Roman trial rather than being handed over to his accusers' jurisdiction.

places.name: Caesarea — Festus's Court
places.text: The formal hearing under Festus follows the same pattern as under Felix: charges brought, none proved, governor politically motivated to please the Jews. But Paul's appeal to Caesar changes everything — the case is no longer in Festus's hands.

places.name: Jerusalem — Renewed Ambush Plan
places.text: The same assassination scheme that failed under Felix (23:12–15) is revived under Festus. The Jewish leaders request a transfer to Jerusalem — knowing assassins wait on the road. Festus unwittingly refuses the plot by insisting the case remain in Caesarea.

tl.name: Paul's Appeal to Caesar
tl.text: Paul invokes his Roman citizen's right of appeal to the emperor when Festus proposes returning his case to Jerusalem. 'I appeal to Caesar!' — the words that set in motion the final act of Acts: the journey to Rome.

refs.note: "As you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome" — the Lord's directive that Paul's appeal now fulfils.
"After I have been there, I must also visit Rome" — Paul's own anticipation, now becoming reality.
"This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar" — confirming the appeal was Paul's own decision.

notes.note: MacArthur on Paul's appeal: 'If I am guilty of doing anything deserving death, I do not refuse to die.' Paul is not appealing to Caesar to save his life but to ensure justice and reach Rome. The appeal is motivated by mission, not self-interest.

"You have appealed to Caesar. To Caesar you will go!" — MacArthur: the human court's decision is simultaneously God's providential direction. Festus acts in self-interest; God acts in redemptive purpose. Human freedom and divine sovereignty operate together.

Calvin on Festus's political calculation — 'wishing to do the Jews a favor': prepared to send an innocent man to a jurisdiction where assassins wait. Political accommodation — doing favors to maintain power — is the constant danger of those in authority.

Calvin on 'To Caesar you will go!': God's providence operates through the legal decision of a Roman governor who has no awareness of fulfilling divine purposes. 'The secret steering of God in things that seem fortuitous' — a legal bureaucratic outcome is the execution of divine plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the appeal was technically to Emperor Nero, who reigned AD 54–68. The formal process required the case to be transferred to Rome with a written summary. Festus will face the problem of what to write (25:26: "I have nothing definite to write to His Majesty about him").

The NET notes that "after Festus had conferred with his council" — the consilium (advisory council) was the formal body required to validate significant legal decisions. The council's agreement confirms the appeal's procedural correctness.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "I appeal to Caesar!" — Robertson notes the formal provocatio ad Caesarem was an absolute right of Roman citizens when denied justice in the provinces. Once invoked before a Roman magistrate, it was irrevocable. Paul invokes it not to escape punishment but to ensure a fair hearing — and to get to Rome.

"They brought many serious charges against him, but they could not prove them" — the imperfect "could not prove" continues the legal pattern from Felix: charges numerous, none substantiated. The Roman innocence chain extends.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener notes the renewed assassination plot: two years after the original conspiracy of forty men (23:12–15), the Jerusalem leaders still plan to kill Paul. The persistence demonstrates both the depth of opposition and the extent of divine protection that has kept Paul alive across two governorships.

Keener provides background on the provocatio ad Caesarem. This right, available to Roman citizens since the Lex Valeria (509 BC), allowed any citizen to appeal to the emperor when facing capital punishment in the provinces. Festus had no legal option but to accept the appeal once invoked.

hist.context: Paul's appeal to Caesar is both a legal manoeuvre and a providential fulfilment. Legally it uses the provocatio ad Caesarem — the Roman citizen's right to appeal to the emperor when provincial justice fails. Providentially it fulfils Acts 23:11: 'you must also testify in Rome.' Paul does not passively wait for God to move him to Rome — he invokes the legal mechanism that will get him there. His active participation in the providential plan is characteristically Pauline.

sections.header: Verses 13—27 — King Agrippa Arrives; Paul’s Case Presented

heb.paragraph: ἄνθρωπόν τινα τεθνηκότα (anthrōpon tina tethnēkota) = 'a certain dead man' or 'some dead man' (the name Jesus appears separately in the verse)

heb.gloss: held over for the Emperor's decision
heb.paragraph: ἐξαιτεῖσθαι (exaiteisthai) = 'to appeal to' or 'to request' (referring to Paul's appeal to Caesar)

places.name: Caesarea — Audience Room
places.text: The akroatērion (audience room/theatre, v.23) — possibly the Herodian theatre adjacent to the palace or a formal reception hall. Agrippa and Bernice enter 'with great pomp' with high-ranking military officers and prominent citizens. Paul is presented before the most impressive human audience yet — the prelude to his most comprehensive speech.

places.name: Rome — Destination
places.text: Festus has 'nothing definite to write' (v.26) — the Agrippa hearing serves the practical purpose of generating a covering letter for Paul's transfer. Rome is not just Paul's destination but Acts' theological destination: the fulfilment of 1:8's 'ends of the earth.'

tl.name: Agrippa and Bernice Visit Festus
tl.text: Herod Agrippa II and his sister Bernice visit Caesarea. Festus describes Paul's case as a dispute about 'a dead man named Jesus who Paul claimed was alive.' Agrippa's curiosity is aroused — setting the stage for Paul's most comprehensive speech.

refs.note: "Felix wanted to grant a favor to the Jews" — the same political calculation that imprisoned Paul under Felix now threatens him under Festus (25:9). The pattern of justice compromised by political calculation recurs.
"This man is not doing anything that deserves death or imprisonment" — Agrippa's verdict after the hearing this chapter sets up.

"They will seize you and persecute you... you will stand before kings and governors, and all on account of my name. And so you will bear testimony to me" — Jesus's prediction being fulfilled precisely.

notes.note: MacArthur on Festus's summary: 'a dead man named Jesus who Paul claimed was alive.' MacArthur notes this is the Roman outsider's summary of the resurrection claim — specific, historical, and completely bewildering to Festus. MacArthur uses this to argue that the resurrection is not a metaphor but a specific claim about a specific dead person who is no longer dead.

"I found he had done nothing deserving death" — MacArthur notes this is the fourth Roman innocence declaration. Luke is systematically building a legal brief: Roman authority, properly applied, consistently finds the Gospel innocent.

Calvin on Festus's description: the governor's bewilderment is theologically instructive. 'Some points of dispute about their own religion' — Calvin notes that Festus cannot see the resurrection as anything but an internal Jewish question. Paul's entire mission is to demonstrate that it is the most universal fact about human history.

Calvin on 'I have nothing definite to write': Festus's administrative embarrassment is God's providential opening. The hearing he organises to generate a covering letter becomes the occasion for Paul's most comprehensive Gospel presentation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the Greek word deisidaimonia (v.19 — "their own religion") is used neutrally here, not pejoratively. Festus is not mocking Jewish religion but describing it from an outsider's perspective. His bewilderment is genuine.

The NET notes that the Roman legal requirement of a written statement (the litterae dimissoriae) was essential for a prisoner transfer. Without specifying charges, Festus could not complete the legal process — hence the Agrippa hearing.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "A dead man named Jesus who Paul claimed was alive" — Robertson savours the irony: Festus has accidentally summarised the entire Christian Gospel in one subordinate clause. His bewilderment is the honest response of the natural mind to the resurrection claim.

"I have nothing definite to write to His Majesty about him" — Robertson notes Festus's legal predicament. Roman law required a written statement of charges when transferring a case to Rome. Paul's actual offence — preaching Jesus's resurrection — is not illegal under Roman law.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener provides background on Herod Agrippa II (27–c.100 AD), son of Herod Agrippa I (who died in Acts 12:23). He was appointed by Rome as king over territories in northern Palestine and given authority over the high priesthood. His intimate knowledge of Jewish politics and religion made him uniquely qualified to advise Festus.

Keener notes the Roman legal requirement of the apostoli (written statement of charges) when transferring a prisoner to Rome. Without specifying charges, Festus could face legal consequences for an irregular transfer. The Agrippa hearing is both a courtesy to the royal visitor and a practical legal necessity.

hist.context: Festus's description of Paul's case to Agrippa (vv.14–21) is Luke's masterstroke of irony. The governor — without intending to — gives the most concise and accurate summary of the Gospel in Acts: 'some points of dispute about their own religion and about a dead man named Jesus who Paul claimed was alive.' Festus is baffled; Paul is preaching the resurrection; and the reader understands that Festus has accidentally described the most important fact in history as a legal technicality.

ppl.name: Paul↗ People
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Festus↗ People
ppl.role: Felix’s successor as governor; sent Paul to Rome
ppl.text: Porcius Festus replaces Felix as governor (c.59–60 AD). He immediately confronts the Paul case, offering Paul a Jerusalem trial — which Paul refuses by appealing to Caesar (Acts 25:11). Festus consults Agrippa II before writing his report to Rome, acknowledging that Paul’s charges concern "their own religion and about a certain Jesus who had died, but whom Paul asserted to be alive" (Acts 25:19). He arranges Paul’s hearing before Agrippa (Acts 25:23–26:32).

ppl.name: Agrippa↗ People
ppl.role: Herod Agrippa II; king before whom Paul defends himself (Acts 26)
ppl.text: Herod Agrippa II is the last significant Herodian ruler, client-king of several territories and a man expert in Jewish customs and disputes (Acts 26:3). Paul’s defence before him in Acts 26 is the most polished forensic speech in Acts. Agrippa’s response — "In a short time you would persuade me to be a Christian?" (Acts 26:28) — is ambiguous: half-moved but unwilling to commit. He agrees with Festus that Paul could have been released had he not appealed to Caesar.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThen Paul made his defense: \ESVPaul argued in his defense, “Neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar have I committed any offense.”NIVFestus, wishing to do the Jews a favor, said to Paul, \ESVBut Festus, wishing to do the Jews a favor, said to Paul, “Do you wish to go up to Jerusalem and there be tried on these charges before me?”

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (c.93 AD)
src.quote: Josephus provides extensive first-century background for the political, religious, and social conditions described in Acts — the Herodian family, Pharisees and Sadducees, Roman provincial governance.
src.note: Acts’ historical details consistently align with Josephus’s independent account of the same period, supporting the narrative’s historical credibility.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS, 1QM)
src.quote: The Qumran community texts illuminate the diversity of Second Temple Judaism from which Christianity emerged — their eschatological expectations, community rules, and temple criticism parallel aspects of the early church.
src.note: The Scrolls demonstrate that the early church’s practices (community meals, shared goods, eschatological expectation) were not unprecedented within Jewish renewal movements.

src.title: Greco-Roman Travel Literature and Epistolary Conventions
src.quote: Acts’ travel narrative (ch.13–28) conforms to the conventions of ancient Greek travel literature, and Paul’s speeches follow rhetorical models documented in Greco-Roman oratory.
src.note: The Hellenistic literary conventions confirm Acts’ composition for a sophisticated Greco-Roman audience and support its historical reliability as ancient historiography.

rec.who: John Chrysostom, Homilies on Acts (c.400 AD)
rec.text: "Chrysostom’s 55 homilies on Acts are the most thorough patristic commentary, emphasising the Spirit’s active role and the apostles’ moral example for the church."

hebtext.note: Paul's appeal in v.11 is the formal legal term provocatio ad Caesarem — available to Roman citizens who believed they were being denied justice in the provinces. Once invoked before a magistrate, the appeal was irrevocable. Paul uses the one legal mechanism available to ensure he will reach Rome.

v.10 — Paul insists he is already standing at Caesar's court (Felix and Festus govern as Caesar's representatives). The appeal is not an admission of guilt but an assertion of his right to a fair Roman trial rather than being handed over to his accusers' jurisdiction.

Festus's summary of Paul's case in v.19 — 'some points of dispute about their own religion and about a dead man named Jesus who Paul claimed was alive' — is the outsider's précis of the entire Gospel. The governor has accidentally identified the most important claim in human history as a legal technicality.

hebtext.gloss: held over for the Emperor's decision
hebtext.note: ἐξαιτεῖσθαι (exaiteisthai) = to appeal/demand examination by the Emperor

thread.text: "As you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome" — the Lord's directive that Paul's appeal now fulfils.
"After I have been there, I must also visit Rome" — Paul's own anticipation, now becoming reality.
"This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar" — confirming the appeal was Paul's own decision.

"Felix wanted to grant a favor to the Jews" — the same political calculation that imprisoned Paul under Felix now threatens him under Festus (25:9). The pattern of justice compromised by political calculation recurs.
"This man is not doing anything that deserves death or imprisonment" — Agrippa's verdict after the hearing this chapter sets up.

"They will seize you and persecute you... you will stand before kings and governors, and all on account of my name. And so you will bear testimony to me" — Jesus's prediction being fulfilled precisely.

tx.title: Western text (Codex Bezae D) vs Alexandrian text
tx.content: Alexandrian(P45, P74, א, B) is the primary scholarly text.Western(Codex Bezae D) is 10–15% longer, with additions that appear to expand and harmonise.
tx.note: Acts has the most significant NT textual variation. The Western text of Acts is not simply corrupt but may preserve early oral expansions. Most scholars follow the shorter Alexandrian text as more original.

tx.title: P45 and early papyrus witnesses
tx.content: The Chester Beatty Papyrus (P45, c.250 AD) is the earliest substantial Acts manuscript, generally supporting the Alexandrian text with some unique readings.
tx.note: The papyrus evidence has largely confirmed the Alexandrian tradition as the best text of Acts, though the Western text’s substantial additions remain a subject of scholarly investigation.

debate.title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.name: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.name: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.

coaching.tip: 'I appeal to Caesar!' (v. 11). The decisive move. Festus must send Paul to Rome. What began as Jewish controversy becomes imperial hearing. Jesus' promise (23:11) advances through legal mechanisms.
'King Agrippa, I consider myself fortunate to stand before you' (v. 26). Festus and Agrippa examine Paul. Roman governor, Jewish king—Paul preaches to power. The prisoner evangelizes his judges.
